quiz_questions.question_text: A 3 kg object moves at 4 m/s. What is its momentum?
A constant force of 20 N acts on an object for 3 seconds. What is the impulse delivered?
A 2 kg ball moving at 5 m/s is brought to rest. What is the magnitude of the change in momentum?
A 0.5 kg ball bounces off a wall. It hits at 6 m/s and rebounds at 4 m/s. What is the magnitude of the impulse on the ball?
quiz_questions.explanation: Taking the initial direction as negative: Δp = m(v_f - v_i) = 0.5(4 - (-6)) = 0.5(10) = 5 N·s.

quiz_questions.question_text: Newton's second law in its general form states F = dp/dt. If p(t) = 3t² + 2t (in kg·m/s), what is the force at t = 2 s?
A force F(t) = 4t N acts on a 2 kg object initially at rest from t = 0 to t = 3 s. What is the final speed?
quiz_questions.explanation: J = ∫₀³ 4t dt = 2t²|₀³ = 18 N·s = Δp = mv. v = 18/2 = 9 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: If the momentum of an object doubles and its mass stays the same, what happens to its kinetic energy?
quiz_questions.explanation: KE = p²/(2m). If p doubles, KE = (2p)²/(2m) = 4p²/(2m) = 4 × original KE.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 1500 kg car changes velocity from 10 m/s to 25 m/s. What is the impulse on the car?
A 0.15 kg baseball is pitched at 40 m/s and hit back at 50 m/s. What is the magnitude of the impulse on the ball?
Two objects have the same kinetic energy. Object A has mass 2 kg and Object B has mass 8 kg. What is the ratio of their momenta p_A/p_B?
What is momentum?

options.text: The rate of change of position
The energy of an object in motion
The product of an object's mass and velocity: p = mv
The force needed to stop an object

quiz_questions.explanation: Momentum is a vector quantity equal to the product of mass and velocity: p = mv. Its SI unit is kg·m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is impulse?

options.text: The instantaneous acceleration of an object
The kinetic energy gained during a collision
The rate at which force changes
The time integral of force, equal to the change in momentum: J = ∫F dt = Δp

quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse is the integral of force over time: J = ∫F dt. By the impulse-momentum theorem, J = Δp.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the impulse-momentum theorem state?

options.text: Momentum is always conserved in any interaction
Force equals mass times acceleration only
Kinetic energy equals momentum times velocity
The impulse delivered to an object equals the change in its momentum: J = Δp

quiz_questions.explanation: The impulse-momentum theorem states J = Δp: the net impulse on an object equals its change in momentum.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the general form of Newton's second law in terms of momentum?

options.text: Momentum equals force divided by time
Force equals the integral of momentum over distance
The net force on an object equals the time rate of change of its momentum: F_net = dp/dt

quiz_questions.explanation: F_net = dp/dt is the general form of Newton's second law. When mass is constant, it reduces to F = ma.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between momentum and kinetic energy?

options.text: Kinetic energy is a vector and momentum is a scalar
Momentum (p = mv) is a vector proportional to v, while kinetic energy (KE = ½mv²) is a scalar proportional to v²
There is no difference; they are the same quantity
Momentum is always larger than kinetic energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Momentum is a vector (p = mv) while KE is a scalar (½mv²). Momentum is proportional to v; KE is proportional to v².

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean for momentum to be a vector quantity?

options.text: It has both magnitude and direction, with the direction being the same as the velocity
It can only be positive regardless of prevailing conditions in practice
It has magnitude only, despite what intuition might initially suggest
It is always perpendicular to velocity under most circumstances involving

quiz_questions.explanation: As a vector, momentum has both magnitude (mv) and direction (same as the velocity vector).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of impulse?

options.text: Newton-second (N·s), which is equivalent to kg·m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse is measured in N·s, which is dimensionally equivalent to kg·m/s (the same units as momentum).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the area under a force-time graph represent?

options.text: The impulse delivered by the force, which equals the change in momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: The area under a force-time graph is ∫F dt, which is the impulse. By the impulse-momentum theorem, this equals Δp.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'extending the collision time' mean and why is it useful?

options.text: Increasing the time over which a given impulse is delivered, which reduces the average force experienced
Increasing the velocity change during impact
Increasing the mass of the objects in the collision

quiz_questions.explanation: Since J = FΔt = Δp, if the momentum change is fixed, increasing Δt decreases the average force. This principle is used in airbags, crumple zones, and padding.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the relationship between impulse and the area under a F-t curve for a variable force?

options.text: Impulse equals the integral ∫F dt, which is the total area under the force-time curve
Impulse equals the slope of the F-t curve
The two are unrelated for variable forces
Impulse equals F times the maximum time

quiz_questions.explanation: For any force (constant or variable), impulse J = ∫F dt, which graphically is the area under the F-t curve.

quiz_questions.question_text: A soccer player kicks a 0.45 kg ball initially at rest. The player's foot is in contact with the ball for 0.02 seconds, and the ball leaves at 25 m/s. What is the average force exerted by the foot?

A 70 kg stunt performer jumps from a 5 m ledge. In one take, they land on concrete (stop time 0.01 s). In another, they land on a foam pad (stop time 0.5 s). What is the ratio of the average force on concrete to foam? (Use g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: Both landings have the same Δp (same momentum change). F_avg = Δp/Δt. The ratio = Δt_foam/Δt_concrete = 0.5/0.01 = 50. The concrete force is 50 times larger.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 1000 kg rocket expels 5 kg of exhaust gas per second at a velocity of 3000 m/s relative to the rocket. What is the thrust force on the rocket?
quiz_questions.explanation: Thrust = v_exhaust × (dm/dt) = 3000 × 5 = 15,000 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a car crash test, a 1200 kg car traveling at 15 m/s hits a wall and stops. The crumple zone deforms over 0.3 seconds. What is the average force on the car during the crash?
A fire hose delivers 20 kg of water per second at 30 m/s onto a vertical wall where it splashes and falls vertically (no rebound). What force does the water exert on the wall?
quiz_questions.explanation: The horizontal momentum of water goes from 20(30) = 600 kg·m/s each second to 0. Force = Δp/Δt = 600/1 = 600 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 0.06 kg tennis ball hits a racket at 20 m/s and rebounds at 30 m/s. The contact time is 0.005 s. A force sensor records the force as approximately triangular in shape. What is the peak force?
quiz_questions.explanation: J = m(v_f - v_i) = 0.06(30 - (-20)) = 0.06(50) = 3 N·s. For a triangular force pulse, J = ½ × F_peak × Δt. 3 = ½ × F_peak × 0.005. F_peak = 1200 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 2000 kg spacecraft in deep space fires its thrusters, producing a force that varies as F(t) = 500 + 100t (in newtons) for 10 seconds. What is the final speed of the spacecraft (initially at rest)?

An airbag deploys in a car crash. A 5 kg head traveling at 12 m/s is decelerated to rest over 0.15 s by the airbag. Without the airbag, the head would stop in 0.003 s against the dashboard. By what factor does the airbag reduce the average force?
quiz_questions.explanation: Same Δp in both cases. F_ratio = Δt_airbag/Δt_dashboard = 0.15/0.003 = 50. The airbag reduces the force by a factor of 50.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 75 kg skydiver reaches terminal velocity of 55 m/s and then opens a parachute, decelerating to 5 m/s in 4 seconds. What is the average net force during the deceleration?
quiz_questions.explanation: Taking downward as positive: Δp = 75(5 - 55) = 75(-50) = -3750 kg·m/s. F_avg = -3750/4 = -937.5 N. The negative sign means the net force is upward: 937.5 N upward.

quiz_questions.question_text: A conveyor belt moves at 2 m/s. Sand is dropped vertically onto the belt at a rate of 5 kg/s. What horizontal force must the belt motor provide to maintain constant belt speed?
quiz_questions.explanation: The sand arrives with zero horizontal momentum and must be accelerated to 2 m/s. F = v × (dm/dt) = 2 × 5 = 10 N.
